Observed facts:
1. At low frequencies, the emitted energy density increases with frequency.
2. For each temperature, the spectrum reaches a maximum at some frequency.
3. At higher frequencies, the emitted energy density falls off rapidly toward zero.
4. The total emitted energy is finite.
5. As temperature increases, the peak shifts to higher frequency and the total emitted energy increases.

Classical assumptions under consideration:
1. The electromagnetic field in the cavity can be decomposed into modes.
2. The number of modes in a frequency interval grows roughly like nu^2.
3. If energy is continuous and ordinary thermal equipartition holds, each mode should have mean energy kT.

u_classical(nu, T) ∝ nu^2 T

This prediction keeps increasing with frequency and leads to an infinite total energy, in contradiction with experiment.

photoelectric_effect_main.prompt:
Experimental observations:

2. Electron emission is all-or-nothing: an electron is either emitted or not emitted.
3. Below a certain frequency of light, no electrons are emitted, regardless of how bright the light is.
4. Above that threshold frequency, electrons are emitted.
5. Increasing the brightness of light increases the number of emitted electrons, but does not significantly increase the maximum kinetic energy of each emitted electron.
6. Increasing the frequency of the light increases the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons.
7. Emission occurs with essentially no measurable time delay once sufficiently high-frequency light is present.

Classical assumptions under consideration:
1. Light is a continuous wave.
2. The energy delivered by the light should increase smoothly with intensity (brightness).
3. A more intense wave should transfer more energy to an electron.
4. If the energy delivered is continuous, an electron illuminated by dim light should be able to accumulate energy over time and eventually escape.

These classical expectations do not match the observations.

Which assumption is most likely wrong? What can explain this all or nothing threshold requirement?

Yet we find they do not follow it with sufficient rigor to be regarded as entirely independent of it. It is also to be noticed that the motion of a part does not come out the same as the motion of the whole. The relation between the two is not that of independence. It is an added relation, due to the fact that the part is initially accelerated and has therefore come into a condition of more rapid motion. The motion of the part is not wholly independent of the motion of the whole, but is added to it. We should expect the motion of the whole to be the same as that of the part, since the motion of the part is the motion of the whole added to an accelerated motion. Yet we find the motion of the part different from that of the whole. This may be due to the fact that the part is initially accelerated, and has therefore come into a condition of retarded motion. It is not at all hard to conceive of a field of force where the motion of a part is independent of the motion of the whole. We shall see, however, that this is not a final field of force. We shall come to see that gravity is an added force, due to the fact that the part is initially retarded. In our final field of force, gravity will be an added force due to the fact that the part is initially accelerated. We can conceive of an accelerating field of force as a field in which gravity is an added force, but we can also conceive of a retarded field of force. The same thing holds true of the motions of parts in a gravitational field. We can conceive of a part moving in such a field with all the motions of the whole. The motions of the part are added to those of the whole, and are not independent of them. We must, then, conceive of a field of force in which the motions of parts are added to those of the whole, and not in which the motions of parts are independent of those of the whole. This is the final field for accelerated motions, and the final field for gravity. We shall see that it is the field in which the motions of all objects initially accelerated are added to those of those initially retarded.

That gravity is an added force, due to the fact that the part is initially retarded, is evident from a simple consideration. The part may be initially retarded by impact. In that case, the part would move in the direction in which the impact is taking place. But, if the part is initially accelerated, it will move in the opposite direction. This is not at all hard to understand. We see that the part is moving in the direction of the impact, and that the part is moving unequally fast in the two directions. The part therefore tends to move with accelerated motion. We cannot conceive of gravity as an added force due to this tendency. We must conclude that the part is moving with accelerated motion at the time the impact occurs, and that the impact is the first step in the process by which the part comes into accelerated motion.

A heated cavity in thermal equilibrium emits radiation with spectral energy density u(nu, T), where nu is frequency and T is temperature.

1. At low frequencies, the emitted energy density increases with frequency.
2. For each temperature, the spectrum reaches a maximum at some frequency.

5. As temperature increases, the peak shifts to higher frequency and the total emitted energy increases.

u_classical(nu, T) ∝ nu^2 T

This prediction keeps increasing with frequency and leads to an infinite total energy, in contradiction with experiment.

sr_approaching_c.prompt:
Suppose there exists a universal speed c that no material object can exceed.

samples.completion:

Suppose we reverse the engine, or let the speed of the object be c, and apply force to it in the opposite direction. What happens? It has no speed left. It has been annihilated by the additional distance it has passed over. We must therefore admit that the speed of a moving object is entirely determined by the force applied to it. Now, this is a very important principle to bear in mind. It is this: that a force applied to a moving object is not the same thing as the object itself. The object is not annihilated. The force has not been applied to nothing. It has been applied to a distance that is the exact equivalent of the speed which the object possessed before. We must, therefore, admit that the speed is entirely determined by the force applied to the object. Therefore, if we apply a greater force to a moving object, we must admit that a part of that force is due to the fact that the object has an existing speed which is not the same as the force applied to it. Now, this is a very important point. It is quite true that the object has no speed when the force is applied to it in the ordinary way. It has apparently no speed, and we have to reverse the engine to get it over the distance that is the equivalent of its former speed. It is evident, therefore, that in this case we have to admit that the speed is entirely determined by the force applied to the object. But, then, the question arises, On what do these various speeds depend? What is the connection between the force and the speed? I think the answer to this question is easy. When we apply a force to a moving object it is either to produce motion or to communicate some power already possessed by the object. If the force is applied to produce motion, then the speed of the object is the exact equivalent of the force applied to it. If, on the other hand, we apply force to a moving object for the purpose of making it communicate some power already possessed by the object, then the speed of the object is the equivalent of the force applied to it. We are thus led to the conclusion that the speed of the object is entirely determined by the force applied to it. We have, therefore, by this train of reasoning arrived at the conclusion that the speed of a moving object is entirely determined by the force applied to it.

5. Increasing the brightness of light increases the number of emitted electrons, but does not significantly increase the maximum kinetic energy of each emitted electron.

1. Light is a continuous wave.

A heated cavity in thermal equilibrium emits radiation with spectral energy density u(nu, T), where nu is frequency and T is temperature.

gr_free_fall_equivalence.prompt:
A person falling freely from a roof does not feel their own weight during the fall.

In a small cabin falling freely, nearby released objects float beside them.

This suggests that, at least locally, gravity can be made to disappear by choosing the right state of motion.

But ordinary forces do not behave this way. They cannot be removed simply by falling with the affected object.

At the same time, a person in an accelerating cabin in empty space would feel weight, and released objects would fall to the floor exactly as in a gravitational field.

How should we think about the relationship between gravity and acceleration?

samples.completion:
It seems evident that we must hold that the force is not removed by falling freely, but that it remains to act in the accelerated motion. We must hold that the force is not a function of the time. We must rather hold that the force is a function of the velocity. The force increases as the velocity increases. This is the law of Lagrange, discovered by him for acceleration. It is not a special force for each particular case, but a general force, a function of the velocity, just as gravity is a function of the height. It is a case of the general function of the velocity, which we call the acceleration of the motion. It is plain that the force is not removed by falling freely. We must, therefore, hold that the force is not a function of the time. We must rather hold that the force is a function of the velocity, and that the acceleration of motion is the product of the velocity into the accelerating force. This is the law of M. and G.

This view is strengthened by the fact that we are able to conceive of the force as not disappearing by falling, as we conceive of gravity as not disappearing by falling. We may conceive of the force as existing in the motion of the body, and as being felt by the falling body, independently of the accelerating motion. We may, therefore, conceive of a force acting on a body from above, without any change in its own motion. We may, therefore, hold that gravity is not removed by falling freely, but that the force remains to act in the accelerated motion. The law of M. and G. is not, then, a special force for each particular case, but a general function of the velocity, which we may call the acceleration of the motion.

The only reasonable way to explain the fact of falling through the air is to hold that the force is not a function of the time, but is a function of the velocity. This follows from the consideration that the force must be the same for all falling objects. Now if the force is a function of the velocity, then the time will not alter with the velocity, but the force will remain the same for all falling objects. It follows, therefore, that the force is a function of the velocity, and that the height is not the only important factor in the change of the force. It follows, further, that the force is not a function of the time, but is a function of the velocity. It follows, then, that the height is not the only important factor in determining the acceleration of motion.

samples.completion:
Is the force removed by falling or by acceleration? Is it a force that can be removed or one that cannot be removed? Is it one that we feel, or one that we do not feel? Is it a force that all objects feel, or one that only objects in the right state of motion feel?

The idea of a force removed by falling is not in accord with our experience in other cases. We are accustomed to thinking of gravity as a force that is felt by all objects moving in a uniform field. We do not think of its removal by falling as a matter of indifference. It must certainly be a force that we feel, or one that we do not feel. Is it a force that all objects feel, or one that only objects in the right state of motion feel?

In trying to answer these questions we are led to the same conclusion as in the case of the displacement of the centre of gravity by falling. We must admit that a force which all objects feel is a real force, and one that we do feel. It is not a force that we only feel, or a force that we do not feel.

It is evident, then, that the idea of a force removed by falling must be modified by the state of motion of the falling body. We may say, then, that the force is removed by the state of motion of the falling body, but only when all objects feel the force. We have here an effect depending on both the state of motion and the presence of the falling body. If we wish to consider the effect without reference to the presence of the falling body, then we must conceive of a force that is removed by falling, but not by any change in the state of motion of the falling body. This is the usual view, but it does not seem to be justified by experience.

One is inclined to think that there is a relation between the time of fall through a very small space and the force exerted by gravity. The time seems to be that required for the object to pass through the small space between the roof and floor. It would seem as if gravity were removed by the falling object passing through the small space between roof and floor. Yet, if we consider the matter, we do not see how this can be the case. It certainly is not a force removed by the falling object. The only reasonable explanation seems to be that the force is not removed by falling through a small space, but only by the state of motion of the falling object, and that the time of fall through a small space is not related to the force exerted by gravity. We should expect to find a relation between the time of fall through a small space and the force exerted by gravity, rather than the reverse.

This is in accord with the other cases where we find a relation between the displacement of the centre of gravity by falling and the force exerted by gravity.

samples.completion:
Is it, like the relationship between distance and vision, a subjective affair, depending upon the state of the retina, or is it a real force between bodies? It seems impossible to explain the relationship between the two states of motion without admitting that gravity belongs to the field of bodies, and that it is a force, as we have called it, acting on the bodies. The only escape from this conclusion seems to lie in the assumption that the force is located in the body itself, and that the space over which it extends is empty. The assumption that the force is located in the body is not necessary for the explanation of the phenomena, but only a convenient way of describing the property of bodies which we call gravity.

This view seems to be favored by the fact that we do not feel the force when we fall freely, but only when we are moving toward something which we expect to meet. The assumption that gravity lies in the body itself seems to be justified by the observations, since the phenomena are not explicable on any other hypothesis.

It is difficult to understand how the force can be located in the moving body, unless it is assumed that the force depends upon the state of the body. It seems to follow, therefore, that we must admit that there is a field of bodies in which the force is located. We are forced to this conclusion if we study the phenomena carefully, even if we do not admit the absolute existence of a force in every particle of matter.

Let us consider the field of bodies more in detail. Suppose we fix our attention on an object moving toward us. What happens? Is the force located in the moving object? If so, then the nearer the object is to the moving object, the greater will be the force. If not, then it will be proportional to the distance between the objects. Now, if the force depends only upon the state of the object, then we should expect it to be the same when the object is very far off as when it is near. But if the force depends upon something located in the object itself, then the nearer the object is to the moving one, the less will be the force. Is it not more reasonable to admit that the force is a property belonging to the moving object, and that it depends upon its state? It certainly must be so if we accept the ordinary view of the field of bodies.

The only escape from this conclusion seems to lie in rejecting the assumption that the force is located in the body. We must therefore admit that there is a field of bodies in which the force belongs to the bodies. This is the same conclusion which we reached when we considered the effect of motion on vision. In this case, too, we must admit that the force is located in the moving body.
